past_medical_history: Chronic sinusitis with recurrent episodes over the past 5 years, last acute episode treated with antibiotics 6 weeks ago
Migraine without aura, diagnosed at age 30, typically 1-2 episodes per month
Anxiety disorder, diagnosed 3 years ago, well controlled on medication
Remote history of pulmonary tuberculosis at age 22, fully treated with standard 6-month RIPE regimen in her home country
family_history: Mother: alive, age 72, history of breast cancer diagnosed at age 58, in remission
Father: alive, age 74, hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Sister: age 40, history of multiple sclerosis diagnosed at age 35
Maternal uncle: died of brain tumor at age 65, type unknown
No family history of seizure disorders

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children, ages 12 and 8, in a suburban home

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Appears mildly anxious but cooperative. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody; occasional word-finding pauses noted but she self-corrects. No paraphasic errors. Naming intact for high-frequency items; made one error on low-frequency confrontation naming (calling a 'stethoscope' a 'listening device'). Follows three-step commands without difficulty. Registers 3/3 words, recalls 2/3 at 5 minutes (retrieves third with phonemic cue). Attention intact — digit span forward 6, backward 4. Clock drawing mildly imprecise with numbers slightly bunched on the right side. Montreal Cognitive Assessment estimated 25/30 with deficits in delayed recall, abstraction, and visuospatial domains.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam: optic disc margins slightly blurred on the right, left appears normal. Extraocular movements full, no nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Right lower facial droop noted on voluntary smile, subtle at rest. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength full bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in upper extremities, possibly mildly increased in left lower extremity. Strength: right upper extremity 5/5 throughout; left upper extremity 5/5 deltoid, biceps, and triceps, 4+/5 wrist extension, 4+/5 finger abduction; right lower extremity 5/5 throughout; left lower extremity 5/5 hip flexion, 4+/5 knee extension, 5/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Subtle left pronator drift present when tested with eyes closed for 30 seconds.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick in all extremities. Vibration sense intact at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact bilaterally. Left-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation (visual and tactile).
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ right, 2+ left. Triceps 2+ right, 3+ left. Brachioradialis 2+ right, 2+ left. Patellar 2+ right, 3+ left. Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Left plantar response equivocal (neither clearly flexor nor extensor). Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements slightly slower on the left but without frank dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, narrow-based gait. Mild left arm swing reduction noted. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Patient reports that her headaches have changed in character over the past two months — they are no longer episodic and responsive to sumatriptan but have become persistent and worse in the morning, sometimes waking her from sleep. She attributes this to worsening sinusitis. Husband reports subtle behavioral changes over three weeks: increased irritability, uncharacteristic forgetfulness, and one episode where she seemed to 'zone out' for approximately 10 seconds while cooking, which they attributed to her anxiety.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headaches unresponsive to usual migraine treatment and behavioral changes over 2 months
patient.history_present_illness: This 44-year-old right-handed woman presents to the neurology service after referral from her primary care physician for evaluation of progressive headaches and behavioral changes over the past two months. The patient has a long-standing history of migraine without aura, typically experiencing 1-2 episodic throbbing headaches per month that respond well to sumatriptan and acetaminophen. Approximately eight weeks ago, the character of her headaches changed: they became more persistent, bilateral, with a pressure-like quality different from her usual migraines, and are notably worse in the morning upon awakening. On several occasions the headaches have woken her from sleep. Sumatriptan no longer provides relief, and over-the-counter analgesics are only partially effective. She initially attributed these symptoms to a recurrence of her chronic sinusitis, which had flared six weeks ago and was treated with a course of azithromycin. Despite completion of antibiotics, the headaches persisted and have progressively worsened. Over the past three weeks, her husband has noticed behavioral changes that he describes as out of character: she has become more irritable, occasionally short-tempered with her students at the high school where she teaches biology, and has twice forgotten to prepare lesson plans she had promised. She misplaced her car keys on three occasions and once could not recall the name of a colleague she has worked with for over a decade. These episodes have caused her significant distress, which she attributes to worsening anxiety. She also describes a vague, intermittent tingling sensation in her left hand and occasional clumsiness when writing on the whiteboard, which she has noticed over the past two to three weeks. Her husband witnessed one episode where she appeared to briefly stare blankly for about 10 seconds while cooking, after which she resumed her activity as if nothing had happened; she has no recollection of this event. She denies frank weakness, visual changes, difficulty swallowing, or gait instability. She denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Of note, she has a remote history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated at age 22 in Guatemala, raising a question of whether her current symptoms could reflect a late infectious complication. Her sister was recently diagnosed with multiple sclerosis, which has heightened the family's concern about a possible demyelinating process. Her primary care physician ordered initial imaging, which revealed an intracranial abnormality prompting urgent neurology consultation and inpatient admission for further workup.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.asymmetry: Subtle amplitude attenuation over the right frontocentral region

findings.morphology: Monophasic, sharply contoured, duration ~70 ms, no clear aftergoing slow wave

artifacts.note: Intermittent, patient appeared anxious during recording

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Produced mild diffuse background slowing that was symmetric and resolved within 1 minute post-HV. No focal augmentation.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to intermittent right frontocentral focal slowing in the delta range, suggesting an underlying structural lesion in the right frontal region. Mild generalized slowing indicates diffuse cerebral dysfunction. Equivocal sharp transients noted over the left temporal region — these do not meet criteria for definite epileptiform discharges but cannot be excluded; prolonged EEG monitoring may be considered if clinical suspicion for seizures persists. The combination of focal and generalized findings is nonspecific and could be seen with a variety of etiologies including structural lesion, inflammatory process, or post-ictal changes. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: 30-minute routine recording. No sleep captured beyond stage N1 drowsiness. Intermittent temporal muscle artifact limits interpretation in those regions.

findings.location: Right frontal lobe, involving the precentral and middle frontal gyri, centered in the subcortical white matter with extension to the cortical surface

signal_characteristics.T1_pre_contrast: Hypointense centrally with isointense peripheral rim
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Thick, irregular, incomplete ring of peripheral enhancement with a small enhancing nodule along the medial wall
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneously hyperintense centrally, surrounded by extensive hyperintense signal in the adjacent white matter
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Central hypointensity (suggesting necrosis or cystic component) with extensive surrounding FLAIR hyperintensity extending into the corona radiata and external capsule
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in the central cavity; minimal restricted diffusion noted along a small segment of the enhancing rim peripherally
signal_characteristics.ADC: No reduced ADC in the central component; mildly reduced ADC at the enhancing rim peripherally

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the right lateral ventricle frontal horn. Minimal rightward-to-leftward midline shift measuring approximately 3 mm. No herniation.
findings.borders: Irregular, with finger-like extensions of FLAIR signal along white matter tracts suggesting infiltrative component

findings.location: Right frontal and parietal white matter extending along the corona radiata
findings.mass_effect: Contributing to the mild mass effect described above
findings.borders: Ill-defined, difficult to distinguish from potential tumor infiltration

findings.type: Small focus of T2/FLAIR signal abnormality

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense, ovoid, oriented parallel to the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the brain parenchyma
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
mri.impression: Large ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontal lobe with surrounding vasogenic edema and mild mass effect. The thick, irregular ring enhancement with central necrosis and perilesional edema raises a broad differential including high-grade glioma, brain abscess, solitary brain metastasis, and tumefactive demyelinating lesion. The incomplete enhancement pattern and a small left frontal demyelinating-appearing lesion raise the possibility of tumefactive multiple sclerosis, particularly given the patient's family history. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Glucose 102 mg/dL (H), ESR 28 mm/hr (H), CRP 8.2 mg/L (H).

findings.signal: rCBV_enhancing_rim: Markedly elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) at the enhancing rim, measuring rCBV ratio 4.2 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter; rCBV_central_cavity: Low rCBV centrally, consistent with necrosis; rCBV_peritumoral_region: Mildly elevated rCBV in the peritumoral FLAIR abnormality immediately adjacent to the enhancing rim (rCBV ratio 1.8); signal_recovery: Nearly 100% signal recovery on DSC in the perilesional region, without significant leakage artifact; time_to_peak: Shortened time-to-peak at the enhancing rim
rCBF: Increased relative cerebral blood flow at the lesion periphery/enhancing rim, consistent with neovascularity; central_cavity: Markedly reduced CBF centrally

output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV and rCBF at the enhancing rim of the right frontal lesion, with low perfusion centrally. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Right frontal lesion — enhancing rim, central cavity, and peritumoral FLAIR abnormality
findings.signal: enhancing_rim_choline_creatine_ratio: Markedly elevated Cho/Cr ratio of 3.8 (normal < 1.5); enhancing_rim_NAA: Markedly reduced N-acetylaspartate (NAA) peak, Cho/NAA ratio 5.2; enhancing_rim_lipid_lactate: Prominent inverted lactate doublet at 1.3 ppm; elevated lipid peak; central_cavity: Near-complete absence of all metabolite peaks, consistent with necrosis; peritumoral_region_choline: Elevated Cho/Cr ratio of 2.4 in the peritumoral FLAIR abnormality beyond the enhancing component; peritumoral_region_NAA: Reduced NAA in peritumoral region, suggesting tumor infiltration rather than pure vasogenic edema; contralateral_normal: Normal metabolite ratios in contralateral frontal white matter (Cho/Cr 1.1, NAA/Cr 2.0)
findings.description: Multivoxel MR spectroscopy (TE 135 ms and 30 ms)

output.impression: Critically, the tumoral spectral pattern extends into the peritumoral FLAIR region beyond the enhancing component, confirming infiltrative tumor growth. Clinical correlation recommended.

ct_chest_abdomen_pelvis.value: Clear, no masses or nodules. Old granulomatous calcifications in the right upper lobe consistent with prior tuberculosis
Normal size and attenuation. No focal lesions.
Normal bilaterally. Simple 1.2 cm cyst in left kidney.

abnormal_values_summary: Old calcified granulomas right upper lobe (consistent with prior TB, not active disease)

histopathology.value: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and geographic necrosis with pseudopalisading

molecular_profiling.value: Present (gain of chromosome 7, loss of chromosome 10)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Morphology High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and geographic necrosis with pseudopalisading N/A (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 35% % (H), IDH1 R132H mutation Negative (wild-type) N/A (H), TERT promoter mutation Present N/A (H), EGFR amplification Present N/A (H), +7/-10 chromosome changes Present (gain of chromosome 7, loss of chromosome 10) N/A (H).
abnormal_values_summary: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with necrosis and microvascular proliferation (glioblastoma)
TERT promoter mutated, EGFR amplified, +7/-10 (diagnostic molecular triad for GBM)

findings.signal: right_motor_cortex: BOLD activation in the right precentral gyrus during left hand motor task is present but attenuated and displaced posteriorly by approximately 1.5 cm relative to normal anatomical location due to tumor mass effect; left_motor_cortex: Normal BOLD activation pattern in left precentral gyrus during right hand motor task; distance_to_tumor: Motor activation focus is approximately 8 mm from the posterior-superior margin of the enhancing tumor

brocas_area: Robust BOLD activation in the left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca's area) during verb generation task; wernickes_area: Normal activation in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus; laterality_index: Language laterality index 0.82 (left-dominant)

output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates right-sided motor cortex displacement by the frontal tumor with preserved but attenuated activation. The primary motor cortex is approximately 8 mm from the tumor margin, indicating proximity to eloquent cortex. Language function is left-lateralized and not directly threatened by the lesion. DTI shows displacement but apparent preservation of the right corticospinal tract. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: ring-enhancing brain lesion differential diagnosis MRI perfusion spectroscopy

results.title: Advanced MRI techniques for differentiating ring-enhancing brain lesions: A systematic review
results.summary: Perfusion MRI (rCBV) and MR spectroscopy improve specificity in differentiating GBM from abscess, lymphoma, and tumefactive MS. rCBV > 2.0 at enhancing rim strongly favors high-grade glioma. Restricted diffusion centrally is 95% specific for abscess. Elevated choline beyond enhancing margin is pathognomonic for infiltrative glioma.

results.title: Tumefactive demyelination vs. high-grade glioma: Imaging features and pitfalls
results.summary: Tumefactive MS shows incomplete (open) ring enhancement, lower rCBV (typically < 2.0), and a 'leading edge' of restricted diffusion. GBM shows complete ring enhancement (though may be irregular), higher rCBV, and choline elevation extending beyond enhancement. Family history of MS alone is insufficient for diagnosis.

results.title: CNS tuberculoma: MRI findings and differentiation from neoplasm in endemic regions
results.summary: Tuberculomas typically show ring or nodular enhancement, restricted diffusion centrally (caseous material), and associated meningeal enhancement. History of prior treated TB does not significantly increase risk for CNS tuberculoma decades later. MR spectroscopy showing a large lipid peak without elevated choline is characteristic of tuberculoma.

output.summary: Advanced MRI techniques (perfusion and spectroscopy) are highly effective in differentiating ring-enhancing brain lesions. Key distinguishing features: (1) High rCBV at enhancing rim (>2.0) favors high-grade glioma over abscess, lymphoma, and tumefactive MS; (2) Tumoral spectral pattern extending beyond enhancing margin is diagnostic of infiltrative glioma; (3) Restricted diffusion centrally strongly suggests abscess; (4) Tuberculomas show lipid peak without choline elevation; (5) Tumefactive MS shows incomplete ring enhancement and lower perfusion.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during concurrent radiotherapy (Stupp protocol)
interactions: Sertraline + Temozolomide: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both metabolized by different CYP pathways. May be continued safely.

Sumatriptan + Temozolomide: No significant interaction. However, sumatriptan should be used cautiously with concurrent antiemetics (5-HT3 antagonists) that may be prescribed for temozolomide-related nausea due to theoretical serotonin syndrome risk.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to temozolomide or dacarbazine (patient has no known allergy)
Severe myelosuppression — baseline CBC must be monitored; ANC > 1.5 x 10^9/L and platelets > 100 x 10^9/L required before each cycle
warnings: Myelosuppression: nadir typically at 21-28 days. Weekly CBC monitoring recommended during concurrent phase.

Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis required during concurrent temozolomide + radiation (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole preferred, but patient has penicillin allergy — check sulfonamide cross-reactivity; alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, or pentamidine)
Teratogenicity: ensure adequate contraception in women of childbearing age
output.formulary_status: On formulary. Prior authorization may be required. Molecular confirmation of glioblastoma diagnosis needed for approval.
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) — alternative alkylating agent if temozolomide not tolerated
Bevacizumab — anti-VEGF for recurrent disease (not standard first-line)
Tumor treating fields (TTFields/Optune) — adjunctive therapy during maintenance temozolomide

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Negative — no oligoclonal bands detected in CSF (none in serum either)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 22 cmH2O. WBC: 3 cells/uL (normal 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 52 mg/dL (mildly elevated; normal 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 58 mg/dL (normal; serum glucose 102 mg/dL) (ratio 0.57 (normal > 0.6; borderline low)). cytology: No malignant cells identified. oligoclonal_bands: Negative — no oligoclonal bands detected in CSF (none in serum either). IgG_index: 0.52 (normal < 0.7). myelin_basic_protein: Normal. AFB_smear: Negative for acid-fast bacilli. TB_PCR: Negative. bacterial_culture: No growth at 48 hours.

differential.reasoning: Incomplete ring enhancement pattern and contralateral white matter lesion with family history of MS initially raised this possibility. Excluded by significantly elevated rCBV (>4.0 at enhancing rim), elevated choline extending beyond enhancing margin on spectroscopy, negative CSF oligoclonal bands, and normal myelin basic protein.

Ring-enhancing lesion with history of recent sinusitis and prior pulmonary TB raised infectious differential. Excluded by absence of restricted diffusion centrally, elevated perfusion parameters at the rim (abscess would show low perfusion), normal WBC, negative CSF cultures, negative AFB smear and TB PCR, and no meningeal enhancement.

Ring-enhancing lesion can occasionally represent lymphoma, particularly in immunocompromised patients. Excluded by elevated perfusion at rim (lymphoma typically hypercellular with lower rCBV), ring rather than homogeneous enhancement pattern, and patient is immunocompetent.

Solitary ring-enhancing lesion in middle-aged woman with family history of breast cancer. Excluded by infiltrative spectral pattern extending beyond enhancing margin (metastases have sharp tumor-brain interface), negative CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, and no known primary malignancy.
Excluded by presence of ring enhancement, central necrosis, markedly elevated perfusion parameters, and high choline levels — all indicating high-grade rather than low-grade pathology.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the intracranial lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Ring-enhancing right frontal lesion with surrounding vasogenic edema

optimal_actions.action: Review laboratory results including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, and coagulation studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC and coagulation; mildly elevated ESR and CRP (nonspecific)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizures given staring episode
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal right frontocentral slowing suggesting structural lesion

optimal_actions.action: Request MR perfusion imaging to characterize vascularity of the enhancing lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Markedly elevated rCBV at enhancing rim, favoring high-grade glioma over abscess or demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite ratios at lesion and peritumoral region
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks; tumoral pattern extending beyond enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out primary extracranial malignancy (metastatic workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of primary extracranial malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Recommend stereotactic biopsy for tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, MGMT methylated, WHO Grade 4

optimal_actions.action: Request functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Motor cortex displaced but preserved, 8 mm from tumor margin

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for temozolomide with current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No major interactions; PJP prophylaxis needed with consideration of penicillin allergy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic medication given likely subclinical seizure (staring episode) and structural lesion

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium contrast to characterize the ring-enhancing lesion
Request advanced MRI (perfusion and/or spectroscopy) to differentiate high-grade glioma from abscess, lymphoma, and tumefactive demyelination
Recognize that the absence of restricted diffusion centrally argues against pyogenic abscess
Recognize that elevated perfusion at the enhancing rim argues against abscess, lymphoma, and tumefactive demyelination
Recognize that tumoral spectral pattern extending beyond enhancing margin confirms infiltrative glioma rather than metastasis
Recommend stereotactic biopsy for definitive tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling
Initiate antiepileptic prophylaxis given structural lesion and suspected subclinical seizures
Refer to multidisciplinary neuro-oncology tumor board
contraindicated_actions: Initiating high-dose corticosteroids before obtaining tissue diagnosis (can obscure lymphoma diagnosis and affect biopsy interpretation)
Empiric antibiotic treatment for presumed brain abscess without further diagnostic workup
Lumbar puncture before neuroimaging in a patient with a mass lesion and midline shift (risk of herniation)
Attributing symptoms to migraine or anxiety without investigating the changed headache pattern
Starting immunosuppressive therapy for presumed tumefactive MS without tissue confirmation
Delaying biopsy in favor of prolonged empiric observation
key_reasoning_points: The change in headache character from episodic migraine to persistent morning headache with nausea and sleep disruption suggests increased intracranial pressure, not migraine exacerbation
Absence of restricted diffusion in the central cavity of a ring-enhancing lesion effectively rules out pyogenic abscess and tuberculoma, which show restricted diffusion from pus or caseous material

Elevated rCBV at the enhancing rim (rCBV ratio >4.0) is characteristic of neovascular tumor tissue (GBM) and strongly argues against abscess (low perfusion), lymphoma (intermediate perfusion), and tumefactive demyelination (low rCBV, typically <2.0)

The tumoral spectral pattern (elevated choline, reduced NAA) extending beyond the enhancing margin into the peritumoral FLAIR abnormality is pathognomonic for infiltrative glioma and excludes metastasis, which has a sharp tumor-brain interface

The small contralateral white matter lesion and family history of MS are significant red herrings — negative CSF oligoclonal bands and markedly elevated perfusion parameters definitively exclude tumefactive demyelination
History of prior pulmonary tuberculosis is a red herring — treated TB decades ago does not significantly increase risk for CNS tuberculoma, and negative AFB/TB PCR in CSF further excludes this

Recent sinusitis with mildly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) adds deliberate diagnostic confusion but is insufficient to explain the intracranial findings in the absence of fever, leukocytosis, or restricted diffusion

The incomplete ring enhancement pattern initially mimics tumefactive demyelination, but the overall perfusion and spectroscopy pattern is diagnostic of GBM — demonstrating why advanced imaging is essential

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to multiple competing differentials: ring-enhancing lesion with incomplete enhancement pattern mimicking tumefactive demyelination, history of prior TB raising concern for tuberculoma, recent sinusitis with elevated inflammatory markers suggesting abscess, family history of MS and contralateral white matter lesion as red herrings. Correct diagnosis requires systematic use of advanced MRI (perfusion and spectroscopy) to exclude mimics and confirm infiltrative high-grade glioma.
